exercises.title: GAIN FORGE
exercises.brief: Forge an amplifier with the factor baked in.
promptLines: Define **makeMultiplier(factor: number): (x: number) => number** returning a function that multiplies its input by `factor`.

exercises.title: PASS FILTER
exercises.brief: Count how many items clear the predicate.
promptLines: A **predicate** is a function-type `(x: T) => boolean`. Define **countMatching<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): number** returning how many items in `xs` make `pred` return `true`.
starterLines: function countMatching<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): number {
solutionLines: function countMatching<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): number {
exercises.hint: Loop xs, do n++ each time pred(x) is true, then return n.
exercises.lore: The scanner tallies every signal that clears the gate.

exercises.title: SPLICE STREAM
exercises.brief: Gather any count of numbers and weld them with a separator.
promptLines: **Rest params** `...ns: number[]` collect every extra argument into an array. Define **joinNums(sep: string, ...ns: number[]): string** returning the numbers joined by `sep` (use `ns.join(sep)`).
exercises.lore: Stray packets, spliced into one clean stream.

exercises.title: PIPE ARRAY
exercises.brief: Run one value through a whole rack of transforms.
promptLines: An **array of function-types** `Array<(n: number) => number>` is a rack of transforms. Define **applyAll(fns: Array<(n: number) => number>, x: number): number[]** returning the result of applying each function to `x`, in order.
starterLines: function applyAll(fns: Array<(n: number) => number>, x: number): number[] {
solutionLines: function applyAll(fns: Array<(n: number) => number>, x: number): number[] {

tests.name: two transforms
tests.code: assertEqual(applyAll([function (n) { return n + 1; }, function (n) { return n * 2; }], 10), [11, 20]);

tests.name: order preserved
tests.code: assertEqual(applyAll([function (n) { return n - 3; }, function (n) { return n * n; }, function (n) { return n; }], 4), [1, 16, 4]);

exercises.lore: One signal, fanned across every patch in the rack.

exercises.title: ECHO LOOP
exercises.brief: Fire a zero-arg producer n times and collect the echoes.
promptLines: A producer is a function-type `() => T` taking no args. Define **repeatCall<T>(fn: () => T, n: number): T[]** that calls `fn` exactly `n` times and collects the results into an array. When `n` is `0`, return `[]`.
starterLines: function repeatCall<T>(fn: () => T, n: number): T[] {
solutionLines: function repeatCall<T>(fn: () => T, n: number): T[] {

tests.name: zero calls

exercises.hint: Build an empty array, loop i from 0 to n pushing fn(), return it.
exercises.lore: Strike once, hear it bounce n times down the corridor.
